topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The clinking of champagne flutes felt like tiny, sharp accusations against the humid evening air. Clara kept her gaze fixed on the intricate floral arrangements, a deliberate avoidance tactic. Every time Marcus laughed—a rich, easy sound that used to vibrate somewhere deep in her chest—a cold knot tightened beneath her ribs. When he finally turned, his eyes swept the room, and for a fleeting second, they locked onto hers. The air seemed to thin out. Clara felt a sudden, unwelcome pressure behind her sternum, a physical tightening that made breathing shallow. She quickly looked down at her glass, the crystal slick beneath her fingers. She needed air, needed distance, and the sight of him, standing so close to the warmth and joy of the celebration, made the space around her feel suddenly suffocating.

[story 2]

"Hey, you look amazing, Sarah," Liam said, clapping her on the shoulder. The forced brightness of his voice did little to settle the tremor in Maya’s hands. She managed a tight smile, the muscles around her mouth feeling stiff and unnatural. Then, across the crowded reception hall, she saw him. David. He was laughing with the groom’s father, his posture relaxed, entirely unburdened. Maya’s internal landscape shifted instantly; the ambient noise of the music seemed to recede, leaving only a high-pitched ringing in her ears. A strange, hot flush crept up her neck, instantly demanding attention. She shifted her weight, subtly angling her body away from the main thoroughfare, focusing intently on the pattern of the marble floor. She felt a desperate urge to melt into the shadows, to simply become invisible.

[story 3]

I watched from the periphery, nursing a lukewarm drink. The music was too loud, the forced cheer of the gathering grating on my nerves. Then, he walked in. Marcus. He moved with that same effortless grace I remembered, and the world seemed to slow down to a sluggish crawl. When our eyes met across the room, the casual camaraderie of the event dissolved. A sharp, unpleasant clench seized my jaw. My palms grew damp; I instinctively gripped my clutch bag until my knuckles were white. Every muscle in my body seemed to lock up, a silent protest against the sudden, unwelcome recognition. I took a slow, measured sip of my drink, trying to anchor myself, but the tremor in my hand betrayed the effort.
